summary.category: India's Biodiversity, Vegetation Types, and Wildlife Conservation
points: India is one of 12 mega bio-diversity countries with 47,000 plant species (10th globally, 4th in Asia), 15,000 flowering plants (6% of world total), 90,000 animal species including 2,000 bird species (13% of world) and diverse aquatic fauna; five major vegetation types are identified: Tropical Evergreen Forests (>200cm rainfall, multilayered, year-round green, Western Ghats), Tropical Deciduous/Monsoon Forests (70-200cm, most widespread, trees shed 6-8 weeks), Tropical Thorn/Scrub (<70cm, Rajasthan-Gujarat deserts), Montane Forests (altitude-based succession from broad-leaf to Alpine), and Mangrove Forests (coastal tidal areas supporting fish breeding).

Diverse wildlife includes unique species like Indian lions (Gir Forest—only country with both lions and tigers), one-horned rhinos (Assam), elephants (Assam-Karnataka-Kerala), snow leopards and yaks (Himalayas), and endangered marine species (gharials, turtles, crocodiles); medicinal plants integral to Indian heritage with 2,000 in Ayurveda (52 critically threatened); approximately 1,300 plant species endangered and 20 extinct due to hunting, pollution, deforestation, and alien species.

Government conservation framework comprises 18 biosphere reserves (12 internationally recognized including Sundarbans, Nanda Devi, Gulf of Mannar), 106 national parks, 573 wildlife sanctuaries, and targeted projects (Project Tiger, Project Rhino); Wildlife Protection Act (1972) provides legal foundation; conservation is essential as every species plays ecological roles supporting crops, medicine, livestock, pollination, and human survival through ecosystem services.
